title: The Body as Home
subtitle: A visualisation for making peace with the body as the one place you always inhabit
excerpt: A short visualisation for moving from body-as-problem to body-as-home. Asks you to walk through the body with the curiosity of someone exploring a house they've been given, rather than inspecting it for faults.
sourceNotes: Written for homemade.education. The body-as-home metaphor appears in somatic therapy and contemplative traditions as a way of developing a more inhabitable relationship with the physical self.

practice.whenToUse: Use when you are in a period of tension or criticism toward the body — comparing, restricting, pushing past signals. Can be used as a daily reset.

content.text: A short visualisation for shifting the relationship with the body. Most of the ways we think about the body treat it as an object to be assessed, a problem to be managed, a standard to be met. This visualisation asks you to try a different frame: the body as a home you actually live in.
Sit comfortably. Close your eyes or let your gaze soften.
Imagine you have been given a house. You have lived in it your whole life but you have never walked through it slowly and with curiosity. You have spent most of the time listing what needs fixing.

Walk through it now. Not to inspect or judge. To notice what's there. Start with your feet. What do they carry you through in a week? Walk up through the legs, the hips, the belly. What does the belly do that you rarely thank it for? Move up to the chest, the breath that happens without your management. The arms that reach, hold, carry. The hands. The face, the eyes, the mind.
This house has been doing a lot. It has been doing it consistently, without being asked.
Stay here for a moment. Not improving anything. Just looking at what you actually have.
Open your eyes when you are ready.

Written for homemade.education. The body-as-home frame draws on somatic therapy traditions and the contemplative concept of embodied presence as distinct from body-as-object. The specific image of the walkthrough as a curiosity practice rather than an inspection practice is original to homemade.education.
